does: This paper investigates the impact of filtering harmful CBRN-related content from pretraining datasets on model safety and capabilities. The authors systematically remove information about chemical, biological, radiological, and nuclear weapons from training data and measure the effects on downstream model behavior, performance on general tasks, and susceptibility to misuse. The work provides empirical evidence on the tradeoffs between safety interventions and model performance, offering practical insights into whether such filtering meaningfully reduces risks without catastrophic capability degradation.
connects: This work extends the growing literature on pretraining data curation and safety-aware dataset design. It builds on earlier efforts to understand how training data composition affects model behavior, including work on filtering toxic or biased content. The paper responds to concerns about dual-use capabilities in language models and connects to research on biosecurity risks, chemical safety, and proliferation concerns in AI. It complements concurrent work on constitutional AI, RLHF, and other post-hoc safety techniques by exploring safety through the earlier stage of data selection.
enables: This research enables more informed decisions about pretraining data governance and supports the development of safety standards for foundation model development. It provides a template for analyzing other categories of potentially harmful training data and could motivate similar filtering studies for other sensitive domains like cybersecurity or financial fraud. The findings inform policy discussions around responsible model release and support building safer AI systems from the ground up. Future work could explore more sophisticated filtering approaches, study the interaction between data filtering and fine-tuning safety measures, and investigate whether removed knowledge can be reconstructed through other means.
